title: Labor pivots to AI intervention
full_text:
Data centre companies could be forced to provide a financial benefit to the local communities in which they operate, as the Albanese government adopts a more interventionist approach to artificial intelligence to assuage voter concern about the impacts of the nascent technology.
The government's shift to being more hands-on than it initially was after the 2025 federal election reflects the speed at which AI is reshaping the economy and testing public trust.
Prime Minister Anthony Albanese is actively involved in developing the new suite of AI policies, according to government sources not authorised to speak publicly.
His intervention comes as unions push state and federal Labor to do more on workers' rights, and Labor MPs face growing local pushback against rapid data centre expansion.
Albanese signalled the government's intention to take a more interventionist policy approach to AI in a speech to the NSW Labor Conference in Sydney on Sunday.
"Right now, the world is queuing up to invest in Australia. They recognise our unique strengths: our people and their skills, our space, our sunlight, our natural resources and our stable democracy," the prime minister said.
"This means that if we act now, Australia can set the ground rules for AI. We can shape the future, not let the future shape us. We can secure new jobs and investment. We can build our sovereignty and our resilience."
The government has made clear that it expects new data centre projects to add electricity supply to the grid to cover all or part of their power usage if they want fast regulatory approvals.
But Labor is now also considering imposing stricter requirements on data centre operators, such as forcing them to provide financial benefits to local communities where they set up operations, rather than mere non-binding expectations.
Assistant Technology Minister Andrew Charlton will argue in a speech to an AI Safety forum in Sydney on Tuesday that stronger regulatory safeguards will not stop Australia from capturing the economic benefits of AI.
With public anger growing around the world about the rapid expansion of data centres and fears about the consequences of the widespread use of AI, Charlton earlier this year warned AI labs such as OpenAI and Anthropic not to make the same mistakes as social media giants.
The Australian Financial Review on Monday revealed that the world's most valuable AI company, Anthropic, wants to buy at least 1.4 gigawatts of capacity from Australian data centres that will cost as much as $US15 billion ($21.6 billion) to build.
Its investment is contingent on striking a copyright deal with the Albanese government to use Australian content to train its AI model Claude here.
While nothing has yet been approved by Cabinet, Labor's more interventionist approach to AI comes ahead of the 50th ALP National Conference in Adelaide later this month, when the party's membership will vote on a new policy platform that says the government "will play an active role in an economy-wide transition" should AI lead to widespread job losses.
While the document stopped short of insisting on union vetoes over use of AI in the workplace, it says workers should be given a role in co-designing AI use.
"Labor believes that working people should be meaningfully consulted when there is workplace change that affects jobs and work, such as the decision to adopt AI and automate, and acknowledges co-design will deliver the best outcomes for both employers and working people, particularly in high-impact use cases," the draft platform obtained by The Australian Financial Review said.
"For this to occur, employers should be transparent about the introduction and use of artificial intelligence and automation in the workplace and meaningfully engage workers in decisions regarding the use of these
technologies."
Deputy Liberal leader Jane Hume on Monday said the platform would hand unions a veto over the adoption of AI.
Hume told Sky News the stronger language in the policy document was "quite confronting" and stood at odds with Labor's pro-AI messaging in parliament. "AI is one of the most exciting technologies that is potentially going to drive productivity in our economy in the future," Hume said. "Labour has made it very clear that it would like unions to have a veto over how the private sector adopts AI."
Hume suggested that unions have the power to compel Labor to adopt their favoured policy.
"The union movement, as we know, has been emboldened by this Labor government, who essentially has rolled over and had its tummy tickled to whatever requests the ACTU seems to have," she said.
Assistant Foreign Affairs Minister Matt Thistlethwaite said the requirement that businesses consult with workers about AI changes was already a feature of the workplace relations system.
"I think that all Australians would expect [businesses to] consult with their workers and their unions when they make major changes that sees the loss of jobs. And that is what this policy reinforces. It's as simple as that," he said.
Australian Council of Trade Unions secretary Sally McManus in June told The Australian Financial Review AI Summit that worker consultation did not need to be overly formal, and unions recognised employers did not always know where they were going with AI.
"It's a matter of just being honest about it, being transparent," she said.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Ronald Mizen | Political correspond
heading: Data Science and AI
